about.title: Pixel voyage
about.agencyDescription: Pixel voyage is a cutting-edge agency specializing in the creation and management of interactive digital art exhibitions. We combine advanced software engineering skills with deep curatorial sensitivity to transform historical archives and physical collections into immersive experiences accessible worldwide.

artist.famousWorkValue: Le Baiser de l'Hôtel de Ville (1950)
artist.subject: Subject
artist.quote: "What I was trying to show was a world where I would feel good, where people would be kind, where I would find the tenderness I hoped to receive."
artist.originsP1: Born in 1912 in Gentilly, Doisneau grew up in a petty-bourgeois environment that deeply marked his vision of the world. Orphaned at a young age, he found refuge in drawing.
artist.originsP2: His shyness led him to photograph objects first, then pebbles, and only later people, capturing "the sought-after moment".
artist.renaultP1: In 1934 he began working as an industrial photographer for Renault. This experience was fundamental for technical discipline.
artist.renaultP2: He was fired in 1939, an event he described as the beginning of his true freedom on the streets of Paris.
artist.resistanceP1: During the occupation, Doisneau put his engraving skills at the service of the Resistance, forging identity documents to help the persecuted.
artist.resistanceP2: In 1944 he immortalized the Liberation of Paris, celebrating the rebirth of joy and stolen kisses among the barricades.
artist.graceDesc: Doisneau sought the instant when the ordinary becomes extraordinary through a gesture, a glance, or a funny coincidence. He favoured the suburbs, the bistros, the children chased from their games.
artist.iconDesc: Taken in 1950, this image is the global manifesto of romanticism. "The truth of a photo lies not in the way it was taken, but in the feeling it evokes."
artist.legacyP1: Robert Doisneau left an archive of approximately 450,000 negatives, a goldmine of collective memory. He received the highest honours, including the Prix Niépce in 1956 and the Legion of Honour in 1984.
artist.legacyP2: Today his work continues to be displayed in the world's greatest museums, reminding us that beauty need not be sought far away, but hides just around the corner.

data.famousWork: Le Baiser de l'Hôtel de Ville
data.subject: Parisian life, Children, Love
data.originsP1: Born in Gentilly, Doisneau turned the streets of Paris into his stage. After training as a lithographer, he found his true voice capturing fleeting moments of beauty in the daily lives of the working class.
data.originsP2: His ability to find enchantment in the ordinary made him the pillar of French humanist photography, celebrating humanity with a benevolent and never cynical gaze.
data.keyProjectP1: Through thousands of shots, he documented the transformation of Paris. His work is not just a chronicle, but a visual narrative that blends reality and subtle poetic staging.
data.keyProjectP2: The famous shot 'The Kiss' has become the universal symbol of Parisian romanticism, embodying Doisneau's ability to crystallize pure emotion in an instant.
data.philosophyDesc: For Doisneau, photographing was not a mechanical act, but a search for 'little lights' in the darkness of routine. He believed that the world was full of daily miracles ready to be discovered.
data.iconDesc: He left behind over 450,000 negatives that tell of an era of hope and simplicity, influencing generations of street photographers worldwide.
data.legacyP1: His images continue to define the collective imagination of Paris. Doisneau did not seek technical perfection, but the emotional truth hidden in a smile or in children's play.
data.legacyP2: His legacy lies in the kindness of his gaze, a constant reminder that beauty resides in the simplicity of human encounters.

data.subject: Identity, Borders, Women
data.originsP1: A mathematician by training, Arthur applies analytical rigor to deeply human narratives. Her work explores the intersections between cultures, with a particular focus on the private lives of women.
data.originsP2: Her career has taken her from Eastern Europe to Saudi Arabia, always seeking to overcome visual barriers to show worlds often inaccessible.
data.keyProjectP1: In 'Jeddah Diary', she documents the lives of young women in Saudi Arabia. In a context where photography is often prohibited, she uses flash and shadows to protect the subjects while revealing their reality.
data.keyProjectP2: The project is a meditation on visibility and the power of the unsaid, redefining the canons of traditional photojournalism through an artistic approach.
data.philosophyDesc: Her work bridges the gap between document and art, focusing on the personal implications of geographical and cultural boundaries and the technological evolution of the human body.
data.iconDesc: A member of Magnum since 2013, Arthur continues to push the documentary medium towards new forms of expression, including experiments with AI and robotics.
data.legacyP1: Through her space 'Fishbar' in London and her global projects, she supports a vision of photography that is both analytical and vulnerable.
data.legacyP2: She remains a fundamental voice in defining how the image can tell the complexity of modern identity in the era of globalization.

data.subject: Memory, Loss, History
data.originsP1: Leaving Japan in 1973, Furuya traveled across Europe stopping in Austria. His work is a bridge between Eastern culture and the European documentary aesthetics of the Cold War.
data.originsP2: His photography became a life diary when he began obsessively portraying his wife Christine, creating an archive of love and pain.
data.keyProjectP1: After Christine's death, Furuya dedicated his life to reprocessing the photos taken together. 'Mémoires' is not just a tribute, but a cathartic process of memory reconstruction.
data.keyProjectP2: The images blend personal tragedy with the historical changes of Europe, making individual pain a universal collective experience.
data.philosophyDesc: Photography for Furuya is the only tool to fight oblivion. His images change meaning every time they are rearranged, reflecting the shifting nature of memory.
data.iconDesc: His photobooks are masterpieces of editing, where every juxtaposition between past and present creates new layers of emotional truth.
data.legacyP1: Furuya has redefined autobiographical photography, showing how the personal archive can become a powerful and heartbreaking art form.
data.legacyP2: His work teaches us that photography does not stop time, but allows us to dialogue with what we have lost.

data.subject: Subcultures, Illusions, Space
data.originsP1: With a background in Graphic Design, Moshammer creates images that explode with color and composition. Her vision moves away from realism to explore surface and illusion.
data.originsP2: Her visual narratives are hybrid, mixing captured reality and aesthetic construction to question the way we perceive places and people.
data.keyProjectP1: In this project, Las Vegas is not a city, but a mirage. Moshammer explores the promises of the American dream through vibrant portraits and almost abstract urban details.
data.keyProjectP2: Her use of saturated color transforms the everyday into something alien, revealing the loneliness and artificial beauty of marginal contexts.
data.philosophyDesc: Investigates how the environment models identity. For her, photography is not a proof of truth, but a means to create new realities charged with emotional and graphic tension.
data.iconDesc: Awarded as FOAM Talent, she represents the new avant-garde that merges photography, fashion and social criticism in a unique visual language.
data.legacyP1: Moshammer challenges the static nature of the documentary genre, inviting the viewer to lose themselves in a world where the border between true and false is intentionally blurred.
data.legacyP2: Her work is a testament to the vitality of contemporary photography in constantly reinventing its gaze on the world.
